title: When designing a GenAI system, how do you decide between relational, document, and vector databases?
prompt:
When designing a GenAI system, how do you decide between relational, document, and vector databases?

*Answer in the editor as you would out loud in an interview (a few sentences to a short paragraph is fine). Then use 💡 Hint, 🧠 AI feedback, or 📗 Show answer.*
reference_solution:
Modern GenAI systems use a polyglot persistence architecture combining structured SQL with semantic vector indexes rather than picking one database for everything. Relational (PostgreSQL with pgvector) is optimal for ACID-requiring workloads — users, billing, audit logs, metadata filters. Document stores (MongoDB, DynamoDB) suit schema-flexible workloads like agent execution traces and logs, where the shape of records varies. Vector databases (Pinecone, Qdrant, Milvus) are built for large-scale approximate nearest neighbor search. In practice, PostgreSQL plus pgvector handles up to roughly 10M vectors alongside structured metadata just fine — dedicated vector databases only start to earn their operational cost once datasets exceed tens of millions of embeddings.

**Takeaway:** Don't default to a dedicated vector DB just because you're doing RAG — PostgreSQL+pgvector covers a large range of real-world scale, and a polyglot split (relational for structured/ACID data, document for flexible traces, vector for ANN search) usually beats forcing everything into one system.
hint_notes: Relational (pgvector) for ACID + up to ~10M vectors; document DB for schema-flexible traces/logs; dedicated vector DB only once you exceed tens of millions of embeddings.
